sources.notes: Shared Div.2 entry; canonical problem is kept as contest 2138B by the Div.1 precedence rule.
Official topic confirms the forbidden permutation-pattern characterization.
Independent writeup used to cross-check the monotonic-stack interval condition.

annotation.summary: Decide when a permutation can be made good under the allowed swap operation.
annotation.constraints: n is large, requiring linear or near-linear detection of the forbidden structure.
annotation.core_idea: The operation preserves a permutation-structure invariant. A bad case is equivalent to the existence of a 321-like forbidden pattern or an interval containment relation between nearest greater/smaller boundaries. Monotonic stacks compute those boundaries efficiently.
annotation.complexity: O(n) or O(n log n) depending on the boundary-query implementation
tricks: Characterize feasibility by a forbidden permutation pattern.
Use nearest greater/smaller boundaries to detect the pattern.
Treat the Div.2 statement as an alias, not a separate problem.

solution_variants.name: forbidden-pattern-monotonic-stack
solution_variants.summary: Use monotonic stacks to compute the relevant boundaries and test whether the forbidden permutation pattern exists.

tags.evidence: The core proof is a structural characterization of which permutations are reachable/good.
The accepted linear solution detects forbidden boundary relations using monotonic stacks.
The forbidden pattern can be checked as containment/overlap of computed intervals.
The correctness proof depends on an invariant of the allowed swap operation.
